questions.question_text: Which of the following methods can be used to charge a metal sphere positively without touching it? Select the most appropriate.
questions.question_html: Which of the following methods can be used to charge a metal sphere positively without touching it? Select the most appropriate.

options.text: Connect the positive terminal of a battery and float the other end of the battery.
options.html: Connect the positive terminal of a battery and float the other end of the battery.

options.text: Rub it with a piece of fur.
options.html: Rub it with a piece of fur.

options.text: Rub it with a piece of silk.
options.html: Rub it with a piece of silk.

options.text: Bring a negatively charged rod near the sphere and touch it to ground for a short while.
options.html: Bring a negatively charged rod near the sphere and touch it to ground for a short while.

questions.correct_option_text: Bring a negatively charged rod near the sphere and touch it to ground for a short while.
questions.explanation: This can be possible by induction, if a negative charge rod is placed near the sphere (at a distance not in touch) than it attracts positive charge inside the sphere towards its side and the negative charges collected on other side and when the surface of sphere connected to earth than the negative charge goes to earth and the sphere remain positively charged.

questions.question_text: If 10 9 electrons move out of a body to another body every second, how much time is approximately required to get a total charge of 1C on the other body?
questions.question_html: If 109 electrons move out of a body to another body every second, how much time is approximately required to get a total charge of 1C on the other body?
questions.explanation: We know that 1 Coulomb = 6.242 ×10 18 electrons Given: 10 9 electrons takes 1 sec {tex} \Rightarrow 1\;electron \to \frac{1}{{{{10}^9}}}\sec {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 6.242 \times {10^{18}} \to \frac{{6.242 \times {{10}^{18}}}}{{{{10}^9}}}{/tex} sec Now, {tex}6.242 \times {10^9}\sec = \frac{{6.242 \times {{10}^9}}}{{60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365}}years{/tex} = 197.93 years = 200 years approx

questions.question_text: Electric charge is a property of

options.text: particles such as atoms, ions and electrons
options.html: particles such as atoms, ions and electrons

questions.correct_option_text: particles such as atoms, ions and electrons
questions.explanation: Every matter is made up of atoms which contains protons, electrons and neutrons. Protons are +ve charged particles and electrons are -ve charged particles. Because of electric charge these particles experience a force in electrical fields.

Two types Positive charge — deficiency of electrons i.e. if a body loses electrons, it will be positively charged. Negative charge — excess of electrons i.e. if a body gains electrons, it will be negatively charged

questions.question_text: Conservation of charges in tribo electric charging
questions.question_html: Conservation of charges in tribo electric charging

options.text: implies half are positively charged and other half are neutral
options.html: implies half are positively charged and other half are neutral

options.text: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged
options.html: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged

questions.correct_option_text: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged
questions.explanation: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged.

options.text: attract each other at times and repel at other times
options.html: attract each other at times and repel at other times

questions.explanation: According to Coulomb's law, unlike charges always attract each other while like charges always repel each other. So two positive charges repel each other.

options.text: charge cannot take any arbitrary values, but only values that are integral multiples of the fundamental charge
options.html: charge cannot take any arbitrary values, but only values that are integral multiples of the fundamental charge

options.text: charges can take any continuous values
charges can take discrete values that are multiples of charge on neutron
options.html: charges can take discrete values that are multiples of charge on neutron

options.text: charges can take any discontinuous values

questions.correct_option_text: charge cannot take any arbitrary values, but only values that are integral multiples of the fundamental charge
questions.explanation: Total charge transferred from one body to other can be calculated by the number of electrons it is known as quantisation of charge given by q = {tex}\pm{/tex} ne, where n = number of electrons, which should be an integer.

questions.question_text: Ionization of a neutral atom is the
questions.question_html: Ionization of a neutral atom is the

options.text: only gain of one or more protons
options.html: only gain of one or more protons

options.text: only gain of one or more neutrons
options.html: only gain of one or more neutrons

options.text: gain or loss of one or more electrons
options.html: gain or loss of one or more electrons

options.text: only gain of one or more electrons
options.html: only gain of one or more electrons

questions.correct_option_text: gain or loss of one or more electrons
questions.explanation: It is not possible to remove or add protons to atom, but electron can be added or removed by an atom easily so charge can be developed on an atom by removing or adding electrons. By adding electrons it becomes negative charged, by removing electrons it becomes positively charged.

questions.question_text: Electrostatic force is the force exerted by-
questions.question_html: Electrostatic force is the force exerted by-

options.text: one charge on another when the two are at rest in a given frame of reference
options.html: one charge on another when the two are at rest in a given frame of reference

options.text: one charge on another when the two are accelerating in a given frame of reference
options.html: one charge on another when the two are accelerating in a given frame of reference

options.text: one charge on another when the two are moving in a given frame of reference
options.html: one charge on another when the two are moving in a given frame of reference

questions.correct_option_text: one charge on another when the two are at rest in a given frame of reference
questions.explanation: Electrostatics means charges at rest,or relatively at rest so this force can be applicable only when charge produce electric field which is constant which is possible only when charges at rest or relatively at rest.

Conductors are materials that permit electrons to flow freely from particle to particle. An object made of a conducting material will permit charge to be transferred across the entire surface of the object. In conductors some electrons are unpaired, so these electrons can move freely.

questions.question_text: In charging by Induction, a metallic object is charged
questions.question_html: In charging by Induction, a metallic object is charged

options.text: by touching with a charged object
options.html: by touching with a charged object

options.text: by bringing a charged object near it
options.html: by bringing a charged object near it

questions.correct_option_text: by bringing a charged object near it
questions.explanation: If a charged object placed near an uncharged object, then redistribution of charge takes place in uncharged object and if we will earth one surface of uncharged object then one charge goes to earth and other charge spread on its surface and it becomes charged.

questions.question_text: For charges q 1 and q 2 separated by a distance R the magnitude of the electrostatic force is given by -
questions.question_html: For charges q1 and q2 separated by a distance R the magnitude of the electrostatic force is given by -
questions.explanation: According to Coulomb’s law, the force between two point charges is The force is directly proportional to the product of magnitude of charges i.e. {tex}F \propto \left| {{q_1}} \right|\left| {{q_2}} \right|{/tex} The force is inversely proportional to the square of distance between two charges i.e. {tex}F \propto \frac{1}{{{r^2}}} \left( {Inverse\;square\;law} \right) {/tex} Combining both {tex}F \propto \frac{{\left| {{q_1}} \right|\left| {{q_2}} \right|}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} ....(i) OR {tex}F = k\frac{{\left| {{q_1}} \right|\left| {{q_2}} \right|}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} ....(ii) Where k is a constant of proportionality known as force constant having different value in different systems {tex}k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {10^9}\frac{{N{m^2}}}{{{C^2}}}{/tex}
The SI unit of quantity of electric charge is the coulomb, which is equivalent to about 6.242×10 18 e (e is the charge of a proton).

questions.question_text: Electric field at a point is defined as
questions.question_html: Electric field at a point is defined as

options.text: electric force experienced by a dipole at that point
options.html: electric force experienced by a dipole at that point

options.text: electric force experienced by two charges at that point
options.html: electric force experienced by two charges at that point

options.text: electric force experienced by a charge at that point
options.html: electric force experienced by a charge at that point

options.text: electric force per unit charge experienced by a unit charge at that point
options.html: electric force per unit charge experienced by a unit charge at that point

questions.correct_option_text: electric force per unit charge experienced by a unit charge at that point
questions.explanation: Electric field intensity of a charge at a point is given by the force exerted by that charge on test charge or unit charge at that point That is, the electric field intensity of charge q is {tex}E = \frac{F}{{{q_0}}} {/tex}

questions.question_text: According to superposition of electric fields, the electric field of any combination of charges is the
questions.question_html: According to superposition of electric fields, the electric field of any combination of charges is the

options.text: vector sum of the individual charges
options.html: vector sum of the individual charges

options.text: cross product of the individual fields
options.html: cross product of the individual fields

options.text: vector sum of the fields caused by the individual charges
options.html: vector sum of the fields caused by the individual charges

options.text: scalar sum of the fields caused by the individual charges
options.html: scalar sum of the fields caused by the individual charges

questions.correct_option_text: vector sum of the fields caused by the individual charges
questions.explanation: When several electric fields are working on single charge then the total electric field intensity on that charge is given by the vector sum of all electric field intensities {tex} \vec E = {\vec E_1} + {\vec E_2} + {\vec E_3} + \ldots {/tex}
